title: My Website Has a Test Suite
excerpt: A Python audit script and headless browser smoke tests run on every push. Here's why a personal blog has a test suite.

title: My Teacher Said I Shouldn't Go To University
excerpt: My high school music teacher told me not to aim for university: I was 'more suited to manual labour'.

title: Free Comments via GitHub Discussions
excerpt: How giscus gives a static site free comments with no database, no ads, and no tracking.

title: Putting My Photos on a Map
excerpt: 239 of my photographs plotted on an interactive world map, this is how the photo map works.

title: Self-Hosting Your Fonts
excerpt: No Google Fonts, no third-party requests, and icon fonts subset from 75 KB down to 1.5 KB. Here's how, and why it's worth an afternoon.

title: Reading This Site Offline
excerpt: This site now works offline: a ~100-line service worker, no framework, no build step. Here's how.

title: The Abandoned Places I Photograph
excerpt: Why I photograph abandoned places and the ethics of pointing a camera at decay.

title: Why I Love LaTeX
excerpt: I wrote my thesis, my papers, and my CV in LaTeX, and I'd have it no other way.

title: What 50,000 Scrobbles Say About Me
excerpt: Fifteen years of Last.fm data - what I listen to when I'm feeling joyful, depressed, doing work, working out or studying.

title: Hosting a Photography Portfolio on GitHub for Free
excerpt: GitHub Pages caps your site at 1 GB, and my photography portfolio is bigger than that on its own. Here's the free workaround: thumbnails in the repo, full-size originals in a GitHub release.

title: Why You Aren't a "Visual Learner"
excerpt: Everyone took the quiz and got a label. Decades of cognitive psychology say the label does nothing: matching teaching to 'learning styles' doesn't improve learning, and believing it limits you. What works instead is effortful, unglamorous, and the same for everyone.

title: Frodo, Sam, and love.
excerpt: A queer-friendly reading of Frodo and Sam: not a hidden romance, but male friendship deep enough for tears, touch, and total trust. Insisting that tenderness must be romance rebuilds the exact wall this story tears down. (Gimli and Legolas, on the other hand...)

title: Optimizing Your Schedule II (or: When Hours Aren't the Thing You Run Out Of)
excerpt: A reply to Part I: time is not the only budget. Energy is a second, renewable resource that some activities spend and others restore. A working model of a depleted week, and why rest is production, not indulgence.

title: What We Leave Behind
excerpt: From the plutonium in the planet's crust to the Golden Record in interstellar space to the memory of a Tuesday coffee, a look at everything we leave behind, and why none of us ever truly vanishes.

title: Optimizing Your Schedule (or: Treating Your Week Like a Solvable Problem)
excerpt: What an operations researcher actually means by optimizing a schedule: objectives, constraints and weightings, a values clarification grid, and a working integer-programming model of your week. Nerd mode optional.

title: What Was I Made For?
excerpt: On finite time, micro-ambition, Gandalf, Tim Minchin, and why every 'one day' dream needs a plan or it disappears.

title: M*A*S*H in the Modern Era: Comedy, Trauma, and the 4077th
excerpt: A modern look at M*A*S*H: how a Korean War sitcom from the 1970s remains one of the sharpest critiques of bureaucracy, war, and trauma in television history.

title: Iain M. Banks and The Culture
excerpt: Smashing the world requires only a hammer; building a better one requires imagination. A reflection on Iain M. Banks's Culture series and the contrarian act of imagining a future that works.

title: The Individualization of Responsibility
excerpt: Corporations reframe systemic crises as consumer morality plays. From plastic bags to five-minute showers, how the public ends up feeling guilty while industrial actors avoid regulation.

title: Advice to My Younger Self
excerpt: Fifty things I would tell my younger self, from pensions and emergency funds to sunscreen, boundaries, and uncomfortable conversations.

title: The Books I Recommend to Friends
excerpt: The thirteen books I actually push on friends, grouped by the kind of friend I'd press them into the hands of.

title: Why Nobody Thanks the Person Who Stopped the Disaster
excerpt: We celebrate the people who fix disasters and take preventive work for granted. That's a bug in how we think, and it costs us.

title: When I Ended Up in Italy Because of Mistaken Identity
excerpt: How a mix-up over an email address sent me to the northern Italian mountains to photograph a tech event I had no business being at.

title: How to Write a Blog (or: What I Learned By Doing It)
excerpt: What I learned about writing a blog: why the word itself put me off, how I found a process, and what to do when people hate your work.

title: The Hidden Cost of Cobalt
excerpt: The batteries in our phones, laptops, and EVs are largely made of cobalt mined in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, often by hand and often by children. A reflection on a presentation I gave, and what we can actually do about it.

title: ISO 8601 and the Date Cult I Happily Joined
excerpt: An ode to ISO 8601 (YYYY-MM-DD) as the only sortable, unambiguous, international date format worth using.

title: Accessibility-First Design in Data Science
excerpt: Data science outputs are often inaccessible by default. Accessibility-first design in plots, dashboards, and tables improves understanding for everyone.

title: The Life and Death of the Early Internet
excerpt: From CRT monitors and Comet Cursor to MSN Messenger, Kazaa, Warcraft 3 forums, and Bebo: a personal history of how the early internet shaped who we are.

title: The Ethics of LLM Use (Not LLMs)
excerpt: An AI researcher's unvarnished take on LLM ethics: the energy numbers, the cobalt hypocrisy, the Jevons paradox, and why telling people not to use AI on environmental grounds is a privilege position.

title: Dungeon Crawler Carl and the Strange Dignity of LitRPG
excerpt: A book snob's confession: how Dungeon Crawler Carl used absurd LitRPG mechanics to deliver one of the most devastating emotional payloads in modern fantasy.

title: From Scotland to Michigan
excerpt: From Scotland to Michigan: a personal account of culture shock, free education, healthcare, xenophobia, weather, and what it means to belong somewhere new.

title: Fifteen Years of Silence: Patrick Rothfuss, The Doors of Stone, and the Architecture of Creative Friction
excerpt: Fifteen years since The Wise Man's Fear and still no Doors of Stone. A personal reflection on the Kingkiller Chronicle, the long wait, the charity debacle, and why writing at this level is genuinely hard.

title: No Idea? No Problem: A Beginner's Guide to Building Your Data Science Portfolio
excerpt: A practical guide to building a data science portfolio when you have no idea where to start. Covers project types, documentation, novelty, collaboration, and working in the GenAI era.

title: Evolutionary Computation's Identity Crisis in the Age of GenAI
excerpt: Evolutionary Computation is having an identity crisis in the GenAI era, despite delivering major real-world wins in engineering, logistics, and constrained optimization.

title: In Defense of Audiobooks
excerpt: The stigma against audiobooks is rooted in bad assumptions about learning, intelligence, and what counts as reading. The research doesn't support any of it.

title: Why It Still Matters to Learn to Code in the Age of AI
excerpt: Coding still matters because the value is not syntax memorization. It is learning to break down problems, reason across systems, and build reliable solutions.

title: Your Professional Second Brain for Local LLM Work
excerpt: A practical system for project documentation, knowledge management, and local LLM workflows that improves delivery, reviews, and project re-entry.

title: Snowball vs Avalanche: The Science of Paying Off Debt
excerpt: Snowball and avalanche both work, but for different reasons. A practical guide with simulator-backed charts on interest, timing, and motivation.

title: Book Ratings Are Broken
excerpt: 569 books rated, and the star system fails at all of them. A data-driven look at my own Goodreads ratings, review lengths, genre bias, and the silence problem.

title: Why Most Self-Help Books Are Trash (And Which Ones Aren't)
excerpt: The bad ones are blog posts stretched to 300 pages. The good ones cite their sources. Here's how to tell the difference.

title: Building a Photo Tagging System with CLIP
excerpt: How I used OpenCLIP to automatically classify 500+ photos into 10 categories in under a minute, zero API cost, running entirely on CPU.
